canonical_summary:
The FCC 5-year deorbit rule is the post-mission disposal obligation adopted in the Second Report and Order, FCC 22-74, on September 29, 2022 in IB Docket Nos. 18-313 and 22-271. It requires that any space station authorized by the FCC, or granted U.S. market access under 47 CFR 25.137, that ends its mission in or passes through the low-Earth orbit region below 2,000 km altitude and plans disposal through uncontrolled atmospheric re-entry must complete that disposal as soon as practicable after end of mission and in no event later than five years after end of mission. The standard is codified at 47 CFR 25.283(e), with conforming disclosure and certification changes at 47 CFR 25.114(d)(14), 25.121(f), 25.122(c) and (d), and 25.123(b).

The rule replaces the previously voluntary 25-year guideline that originated in the IADC Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines and the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices. After OMB approval of the information collections on July 2, 2024 (89 FR 64715, August 9, 2024), the disclosure obligations became operative for applications filed on or after September 29, 2024. Satellites authorized before September 29, 2022 and launched before September 29, 2024 remain subject to the prior 25-year benchmark; all other systems are bound by the five-year clock and must show probability of successful post-mission disposal of at least 0.9.
meta_description: The FCC 5-year deorbit rule (FCC 22-74, IB Docket 18-313) shortens LEO post-mission disposal from 25 years to 5. Scope, 47 CFR 25.283(e), compliance paths.

sections.heading: What the rule is: FCC 22-74 and the move from 25 years to 5
paragraphs: The FCC 5-year deorbit rule is the substantive holding of the Second Report and Order in the matter of Space Innovation; Mitigation of Orbital Debris in the New Space Age, released as FCC 22-74. The Commission adopted the Order on September 29, 2022 and released it on September 30, 2022 in IB Docket Nos. 18-313 and 22-271. Publication in the Federal Register followed on December 2, 2022 at 87 FR 74006. Office of Management and Budget approval of the modified information collections under the Paperwork Reduction Act issued on July 2, 2024, and the new disclosure obligations became operative for applications filed on or after September 29, 2024, two years after adoption.

The Order replaced the 25-year post-mission disposal benchmark that had governed industry practice since the IADC Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines and the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (ODMSP, last revised November 2019). The 25-year figure had been cited in compliance showings but was never a binding FCC rule. FCC 22-74 makes the new five-year obligation a certified, enforceable element of every Part 25 space-station application and every grant of U.S. market access under 47 CFR 25.137.
bullets: Title: Mitigation of Orbital Debris in the New Space Age, Second Report and Order
OMB / PRA approval: July 2, 2024 (89 FR 64715, August 9, 2024)
Operative for applications filed on or after September 29, 2024
Prior benchmark: 25 years, per IADC guidelines and the 2019 ODMSP

sections.heading: The rule text: 47 CFR 25.283(e), 25.114, 25.121, 25.122, and 25.123
paragraphs: The five-year obligation is codified in the disposal section of Part 25, with conforming changes throughout the application-and-certification provisions. Section 25.283(e) reaches space stations ending their mission in, or passing through, the LEO region below 2,000 km altitude that plan disposal through uncontrolled atmospheric re-entry. Disposal must be completed as soon as practicable following end of mission, and no later than five years after the end of mission. End of mission, defined in FCC 22-74 at paragraphs 18 to 19, is the point at which a maneuverable spacecraft is no longer capable of performing collision-avoidance maneuvers, or for non-maneuverable spacecraft the completion of the primary mission.

Four certification and disclosure provisions carry the rule across every Part 25 licensing pathway, so that the streamlined small-sat routes cannot be used to avoid it. Conforming changes also reach 47 CFR 5.64(b)(7)(iv)(A) for experimental authorizations and 47 CFR 97.207(g)(1)(vii)(D)(1) for amateur satellites.

subsections.heading: 47 CFR 25.114(d)(14): orbital-debris disclosure in space-station applications
paragraphs: Section 25.114(d)(14) is the orbital-debris mitigation disclosure required of every Part 25 space-station applicant. As revised, applicants seeking authorization in or transiting LEO must describe how the post-mission disposal plan satisfies the five-year completion requirement of 25.283(e), rather than relying on the former 25-year benchmark.

subsections.heading: 47 CFR 25.121(f): license term and renewals
paragraphs: Section 25.121(f) ties license-term and renewal showings to the new disposal rule. An operator cannot extend or renew authority for a LEO system without a disposal plan that satisfies the five-year clock measured from end of mission for each individual spacecraft.

subsections.heading: 47 CFR 25.122(c) and (d): streamlined small space station authorization
paragraphs: Section 25.122 governs the streamlined small space station path. The five-year disposal obligation now sits alongside the existing certifications, including the probability of successful disposal of 0.9 or greater at 25.122(d)(9) and the maximum probability of collision with large objects of 0.001 at 25.122(c)(7).

subsections.heading: 47 CFR 25.123(b): streamlined small spacecraft authorization
paragraphs: Section 25.123 is the smaller, even more streamlined small-spacecraft pathway adopted in 2019. The Order amends 25.123(b) so its required showings incorporate the five-year completion-of-disposal standard for LEO systems.

sections.heading: Applicability: who is captured, who is exempt, grandfathering
paragraphs: Section 25.283(e) reaches every system the FCC licenses or to which it grants U.S. market access. That includes Part 25 commercial NGSO satellite licensees, Part 5 experimental authorizations under 5.64, Part 97 amateur satellite operators under 97.207, earth-station applicants communicating with a non-U.S.-licensed space station, and petitioners for declaratory ruling under 47 CFR 25.137. Geostationary terminations that transit LEO during disposal are captured by the passing through clause.

Non-U.S.-licensed operators are not exempt. FCC 22-74 at paragraphs 41 to 47 created an alternative compliance path: a foreign operator may show that its debris-mitigation plan is subject to direct and effective regulatory oversight by the national licensing authority of the licensing country. Operators that cannot make that showing must comply with 25.283(e) on the same five-year timeline as U.S. licensees. ITU-coordinated systems that do not seek FCC market access are outside the rule's jurisdictional reach, a gap the Commission acknowledged.

subsections.heading: The 2,000 km boundary
paragraphs: The 2,000 km figure preserves the historical IADC and NASA boundary of the protected LEO region. A spacecraft that disposes by raising to a graveyard orbit above LEO, or one that operates entirely in MEO or GEO and never traverses sub-2,000 km altitude during disposal, falls outside subsection (e). A GEO or MEO satellite that re-enters through the LEO band during uncontrolled disposal is captured by the passing through clause and must clear within five years of end of mission.

subsections.heading: Grandfathering: the September 29, 2022 to September 29, 2024 window
paragraphs: The Commission adopted a two-year transition window. Satellites authorized by the FCC before September 29, 2022 and launched before September 29, 2024 remain subject to the prior 25-year guideline. Satellites authorized before September 29, 2022 but not launched until after September 29, 2024 must comply with the five-year rule. All applications filed or granted on or after September 29, 2022, and all launches on or after September 29, 2024, are subject to 25.283(e). The Commission preserved discretion to revisit the grandfathered cohort if debris conditions warrant.

sections.heading: Compliance paths: passive decay, controlled reentry, graveyard orbit
paragraphs: Three disposal architectures satisfy the post-mission disposal obligation. The choice is driven by operating altitude, vehicle mass and survivability, and propellant budget.

subsections.heading: Passive atmospheric decay
paragraphs: The spacecraft is left on an orbit whose perigee is low enough that residual atmospheric drag removes it within the five-year window measured from end of mission. The operator must demonstrate decay with conservative solar-activity projections, typically using the NASA Debris Assessment Software (DAS) coupled to ORDEM flux models. Above roughly 550 to 600 km, natural decay alone is unlikely to satisfy the five-year clock, forcing propulsion, drag augmentation, or lower operating altitudes.

subsections.heading: Controlled reentry
paragraphs: The vehicle uses onboard propulsion for a targeted, near-instantaneous deorbit, normally into the South Pacific Ocean Uninhabited Area at Point Nemo. NASA-STD-8719.14 requires controlled reentry whenever the expected casualty risk from uncontrolled reentry exceeds 1 in 10,000 (Ec greater than 1e-4). ORSAT is the reference survivability code. The FCC adopted the same 1e-4 casualty threshold in its 2020 Order, FCC 20-54.

subsections.heading: Graveyard orbit
paragraphs: Where atmospheric reentry is energetically impractical, the vehicle is raised into a disposal orbit clear of the protected region. The IADC formula for the minimum perigee above the GEO ring is delta_H = 235 km + 1000 * Cr * (A/m), where Cr is the solar radiation pressure coefficient and A/m is the area-to-mass ratio. Typical disposal altitudes land 300 to 400 km above GEO. The FCC five-year rule does not reach pure GEO disposal; GEO operators file under the GEO-specific provisions of section 25.283 and coordinate the end-of-life maneuver through the ITU.

sections.heading: Design implications and disposal reliability
paragraphs: The five-year rule operates together with two long-standing numeric thresholds. The probability of successful post-mission disposal must be at least 0.9 per spacecraft, per 47 CFR 25.122(d)(9) and the 2019 ODMSP. The probability of collision with large (10 cm or greater) tracked debris must be no more than 0.001 over the spacecraft's orbital lifetime, per 25.122(c)(7) and NASA-STD-8719.14. The Commission has signaled that constellation operators should target 0.99 or better disposal reliability, and the FCC requested showings on aggregate expected debris generation in IB Docket 22-271.

The 0.9 figure traces to ISO 24113 and is mirrored in NASA-STD-8719.14B and the IADC guidelines. ESA's Space Debris Mitigation Requirements ESSB-ST-U-007, issued November 2023, tightened the regime further for ESA missions by combining a greater than 0.9 disposal success probability with a five-year LEO clearance window and explicit passivation, health monitoring, and collision-avoidance interface requirements.

subsections.heading: Residual propellant and delta-V margin
paragraphs: ISO 24113 defines the disposal probability as the joint probability of completing all disposal actions, including subsystem availability, propellant availability and gauging uncertainty, ground-segment availability, and operational remediation of in-flight degradations. Operators must reserve delta-V for nominal disposal plus 3-sigma navigation, insertion error, and orbit-maintenance dispersions, then add margin for dynamic residual, vapor pressure, and ullage trapped at the post-mission disposal attitude. PVT gauging uncertainty alone can absorb 5 to 10 percent of usable propellant, which must be reflected in the 0.9 calculation. For a 12U with a green-propellant thruster delivering 100 to 150 m/s wet, the disposal plus residual reservation can claim 30 to 60 percent of the wet delta-V budget once five-year clearance is enforced from altitudes above 550 km.

subsections.heading: End-of-mission activation reliability
paragraphs: To reach 0.9 end to end, the activation chain (FDIR detects end of mission, attitude is acquired, thruster fires, passivation sequence completes) must be near-deterministic. Design responses include autonomous EOM trigger logic on the onboard computer independent of ground command, watchdog timeouts that initiate disposal after loss of contact, redundant or cross-strapped reaction wheels and thruster valves on platforms above 6U, dual-string avionics on the disposal-critical path, and mechanically committed passivation that depletes tanks and batteries, isolates battery strings, and vents pressurant.

subsections.heading: Net effect on bus design
paragraphs: Altitude selection has become a propulsion-sizing decision. Above 550 to 600 km the five-year rule forces active propulsion or a deployable drag device; below 450 km the rule can still be met passively. Demise-and-survivability analyses indicate that adding passivation valves, redundant inhibits, deployable drag devices, and gauging hardware adds 5 to 15 percent dry mass on a CubeSat-class bus. Post-mission disposal has moved from a checklist item to a top-level systems-engineering driver, on the same footing as power and link budgets.

sections.heading: Enforcement risk and non-Part-25 operators
paragraphs: Enforcement attaches at the application-and-certification stage. Section 25.114(d)(14) makes the disposal showing a certified statement, and the streamlined small-sat certifications at 25.122 and 25.123 carry the same legal weight. A material misstatement exposes the operator to license revocation, denial of renewal under 25.121(f), forfeitures, and referral for enforcement under sections 312 and 503 of the Communications Act. License-term and renewal showings under 25.121(f) function as a periodic compliance gate: an operator cannot extend or renew authority for a LEO system whose disposal plan does not satisfy the five-year clock.

For non-Part-25 operators, the same substantive standard reaches Part 5 experimental authorizations through 5.64(b)(7)(iv)(A) and Part 97 amateur satellites through 97.207(g)(1)(vii)(D)(1). Foreign operators are reached through 47 CFR 25.137: an earth-station applicant communicating with a non-U.S. space station, or a petitioner for declaratory ruling, must demonstrate that the space station satisfies the FCC's orbital-debris-mitigation requirements, including 25.283(e), or qualify under the home-regulator alternative described in FCC 22-74 paragraphs 41 to 47. ITU-coordinated systems with no U.S. market access remain outside the FCC's jurisdictional reach, though they remain subject to ITU-R S.1003-2 for GEO disposal, WRC-19 Resolution 35 milestone reporting for NGSO systems, and the licensing authority of the notifying administration.

faqs.question: What is the FCC 5-year deorbit rule?
faqs.answer: The FCC 5-year deorbit rule is the post-mission disposal requirement adopted in the Second Report and Order, FCC 22-74, in IB Docket Nos. 18-313 and 22-271 on September 29, 2022. It is codified at 47 CFR 25.283(e) and requires that any space station the FCC authorizes or grants U.S. market access to, which ends its mission in or passes through the LEO region below 2,000 km altitude and plans disposal by uncontrolled atmospheric re-entry, must complete that disposal as soon as practicable after end of mission and no later than five years after end of mission.

faqs.question: When did the FCC 5-year deorbit rule take effect?
faqs.answer: The Order was adopted September 29, 2022 and released September 30, 2022, with Federal Register publication on December 2, 2022 at 87 FR 74006. Office of Management and Budget approval of the modified information collections under the Paperwork Reduction Act issued on July 2, 2024 (89 FR 64715, August 9, 2024). The disclosure and certification obligations became operative for applications filed on or after September 29, 2024, the close of the two-year transition window the Commission set in FCC 22-74.

faqs.question: Which satellites are grandfathered under the prior 25-year benchmark?
faqs.answer: Satellites authorized by the FCC before September 29, 2022 and launched before September 29, 2024 remain subject to the prior 25-year guideline that had governed industry practice. Satellites authorized before September 29, 2022 but not launched until after September 29, 2024 must comply with the five-year rule. All applications filed or granted on or after September 29, 2022, and all launches on or after September 29, 2024, are subject to 25.283(e). The Commission preserved discretion to revisit the grandfathered cohort if debris conditions warrant.

faqs.question: Does the FCC 5-year deorbit rule apply to non-U.S.-licensed satellites?
faqs.answer: Yes, to the extent they seek U.S. market access. Under 47 CFR 25.137, any earth-station applicant communicating with a non-U.S. space station, and any petitioner for declaratory ruling for market access, must demonstrate compliance with the FCC's orbital-debris-mitigation requirements, including 25.283(e). FCC 22-74 paragraphs 41 to 47 created an alternative pathway under which a foreign operator may instead show that its debris-mitigation plan is subject to direct and effective regulatory oversight by the national licensing authority of the licensing country.

faqs.question: Does the rule apply to satellites operating above 2,000 km altitude?
faqs.answer: Not directly. Section 25.283(e) is by its terms limited to space stations ending their mission in, or passing through, the LEO region below 2,000 km altitude that plan disposal by uncontrolled atmospheric re-entry. A spacecraft disposed by raising into a graveyard orbit above LEO, or one operating entirely in MEO or GEO that never traverses sub-2,000 km altitude during disposal, falls outside subsection (e). However, a GEO or MEO satellite that re-enters through the LEO band during uncontrolled disposal is captured by the passing through clause and must clear within five years.

faqs.question: What disposal probability must operators demonstrate?
faqs.answer: The probability of successful post-mission disposal must be at least 0.9 per spacecraft, certified under 47 CFR 25.122(d)(9) and consistent with the 2019 U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices. The Commission has signaled that constellation operators should target 0.99 or better and requested showings on aggregate expected debris generation in IB Docket 22-271. The 0.9 figure traces to ISO 24113 and is mirrored in NASA-STD-8719.14B; it is the joint probability of completing all disposal actions, including subsystem availability, propellant availability and gauging uncertainty, and ground-segment availability.

faqs.question: What is the casualty-risk threshold for controlled reentry?
faqs.answer: The expected casualty risk from uncontrolled reentry must not exceed 1 in 10,000, expressed as Ec less than or equal to 1e-4. The threshold originates in NASA-STD-8719.14 and was adopted by the FCC in the 2020 Order on orbital debris (FCC 20-54). Where the analyzed casualty risk exceeds the threshold, the operator must perform a controlled reentry, typically into the South Pacific Ocean Uninhabited Area at Point Nemo, using ORSAT or an equivalent survivability code to size the debris footprint and demonstrate compliance.

faqs.question: What is the large-object collision threshold?
faqs.answer: The probability of collision with a tracked object 10 cm or larger over the spacecraft's orbital lifetime must be no greater than 0.001, certified under 47 CFR 25.122(c)(7) and NASA-STD-8719.14. The figure is evaluated using the NASA Debris Assessment Software (DAS) coupled to the ORDEM debris flux model. It is a per-mission cap, evaluated per spacecraft. For constellations the FCC also examines the product of disposal reliability and fleet size to determine the expected number of failed disposals.

faqs.question: Which Part 25 provisions were amended by FCC 22-74?
faqs.answer: FCC 22-74 substantively amends 47 CFR 25.283 to add subsection (e), the five-year disposal obligation. Conforming changes update the application and certification provisions: 47 CFR 25.114(d)(14) for the orbital-debris disclosure in space-station applications, 47 CFR 25.121(f) for license term and renewals, 47 CFR 25.122(c) and (d) for the streamlined small space station path, and 47 CFR 25.123(b) for the streamlined small spacecraft path. Parallel changes reach Part 5 experimental authorizations at 5.64(b)(7)(iv)(A) and Part 97 amateur satellites at 97.207(g)(1)(vii)(D)(1).

faqs.question: How does the rule interact with ITU coordination?
faqs.answer: The ITU Radio Regulations do not impose end-of-life disposal obligations directly; their leverage is over spectrum and orbital slot rights. Recommendation ITU-R S.1003-2 is the principal ITU instrument on GEO disposal and endorses the IADC re-orbit altitude formula. WRC-19 Resolution 35 requires administrations to confirm that non-GSO systems can be maintained and that orbital-debris-mitigation measures are accounted for in milestone reporting (10, 50, and 90 percent deployment milestones over 7 years). Enforcement of post-mission disposal remains with national licensing authorities, including the FCC.

faqs.question: How is end of mission defined for purposes of the five-year clock?
faqs.answer: End of mission is defined in FCC 22-74 paragraphs 18 to 19. For spacecraft with collision-avoidance capability, end of mission is the moment the spacecraft is no longer capable of performing collision-avoidance maneuvers. For spacecraft without that capability, end of mission is when the spacecraft completes its primary mission, for example communications or remote sensing. The definition prevents operators from gaming the start of the disposal clock by keeping a derelict spacecraft nominally in mission, and aligns FCC practice with the ISO 24113 and IADC framing.

faqs.question: What design changes does the rule force on CubeSats and small satellites?
faqs.answer: Above 550 to 600 km the five-year window forces active propulsion or a deployable drag device, because natural decay alone is unlikely to clear in time. Below 450 km the window can still be met passively. Demise-and-survivability analyses indicate 5 to 15 percent added dry mass on a CubeSat-class bus from passivation valves, redundant inhibits, deployable drag devices, and gauging hardware. The disposal burn must be sized from any plausible failure epoch, not just nominal end of mission, and PVT gauging uncertainty of 5 to 10 percent must be reflected in the 0.9 disposal-reliability calculation.
